symptom: SMTP server hosted in Azure receives '554: No PTR Record' error when sending or receiving messages from remote mail servers
rootCause: Azure public IP reverse DNS (PTR) records must be configured using -ReverseFqdn property on the PublicIpAddress resource, not in custom domain zones. If reverse FQDN does not belong to the subscription, configuration fails.
solution: Set -ReverseFqdn on the PublicIpAddress resource via Set-AzPublicIpAddress. Ensure IP and reverse FQDN belong to the same subscription. If SMTP banner is manually changed to match default reverse FQDN, remote servers may still fail if SMTP banner host doesn't match MX record.

symptom: Network traffic unexpectedly blocked despite having an NSG allow rule - a higher-priority deny rule (lower priority number) overrides the allow rule
rootCause: NSG rules are evaluated by priority from lowest number to highest. A deny rule with priority 500 blocks traffic before an allow rule with priority 1000 is evaluated.
solution: Review all rules sorted by priority. Either remove the conflicting deny rule, change the allow rule priority to a lower number than the deny rule, or narrow the scope of the deny rule to exclude desired traffic.

symptom: Traffic blocked when NSGs applied at both subnet-level and NIC-level - one NSG allows traffic but the other blocks it
rootCause: When NSGs are applied at both subnet and NIC levels, BOTH must allow the traffic. For inbound: subnet NSG evaluated first, then NIC NSG. Missing allow rule in either NSG causes traffic to be denied by default DenyAllInBound rule.
solution: Use Effective Security Rules view to identify which NSG is blocking. Add matching allow rules in both subnet-level and NIC-level NSGs. Simplify by removing NSG from either subnet or NIC if dual NSGs aren't needed.

symptom: Traffic from certain source IPs blocked while traffic from other sources in the same VNet works - NSG rule has restrictive source IP range
rootCause: NSG allow rule specifies a source IP range (e.g., 10.0.1.0/24) that doesn't include the connecting VM's subnet (e.g., 10.0.2.0/24). Traffic falls through to default deny rule.
solution: Verify the source IP of the connecting machine. Update the rule's source IP range to include the correct address, or use service tag like VirtualNetwork. Be aware that VirtualNetwork tag includes peered networks and on-premises connections through gateways.

symptom: NVA (Network Virtual Appliance) not forwarding traffic - VM or VPN connectivity errors when using partner NVA in Azure
rootCause: IP forwarding not enabled on the NVA's network interface. Without IP forwarding, the NVA drops packets not destined for its own IP address.
solution: Enable IP forwarding on NVA NIC: Portal > NVA resource > Networking > Network interface > IP configuration > enable IP forwarding. Or via PowerShell: Get-AzNetworkInterface, set EnableIPForwarding = 1, Set-AzNetworkInterface. Also verify UDRs point traffic to NVA and NSG allows traffic when using Standard SKU public IP.

symptom: SSH connection to Azure Linux VM times out or returns 'Connection refused' / 'Connection timed out' - no inbound NSG rule allows TCP port 22
rootCause: When VM is created without selecting SSH as allowed inbound port, the default DenyAllInBound rule (priority 65500) blocks all SSH connections from outside the virtual network.
solution: Add inbound NSG allow rule: Source=appropriate IP/range/service-tag, Destination port=22, Protocol=TCP, Action=Allow, Priority lower than any conflicting deny rule. Apply rule in BOTH subnet and NIC NSGs if both exist. Consider Azure Bastion or JIT access for production.

symptom: SSH connection fails despite NSG rule allowing port 22 - sshd configured to listen on a custom non-standard port (e.g., 2222) but NSG rule only allows port 22
rootCause: Linux VM's SSH daemon (sshd) is configured via /etc/ssh/sshd_config to listen on a non-standard port, but the NSG allow rule only permits traffic on default port 22.
solution: Check sshd_config for Port directive: grep -i port /etc/ssh/sshd_config. Update NSG rule destination port to match the configured SSH port. Update automation scripts and connection strings to use the custom port.

symptom: Azure VM cannot send outbound email on SMTP port 25 - connection times out or is blocked for non-Enterprise Agreement subscriptions
rootCause: Azure platform blocks outbound SMTP connections on TCP port 25 for all subscription types except Enterprise Agreement (EA) and Microsoft Customer Agreement for enterprise (MCA-E). Enterprise Dev/Test subscriptions also blocked by default.
solution: Use authenticated SMTP relay service (e.g., Azure Communication Services) on TCP port 587 instead of port 25. For EA/MCA-E subscriptions, port 25 is allowed but delivery depends on recipient domain acceptance. For Enterprise Dev/Test, request unblock via Azure portal Diagnose and Solve.

symptom: Azure VM cannot connect to the second (secondary) network adapter of another VM in the same VNet - traffic limited to same subnet only
rootCause: Secondary network interfaces are not configured with a default gateway by default. Without a gateway route entry, traffic flow on the secondary adapter is limited to the same subnet.
solution: On the VM with secondary NIC, add a persistent route: Route add 0.0.0.0 mask 0.0.0.0 -p <Gateway IP> where Gateway IP is the first usable IP of the secondary NIC subnet. Verify with route print. Also check NSG rules on both primary and secondary NICs.

symptom: Intermittent VM connectivity failures when behind a load balancer - connections drop or time out periodically due to SNAT port exhaustion
rootCause: VMs behind a load balancer using SNAT for outbound connections can exhaust the available SNAT ports, causing intermittent connection failures. Each outbound connection consumes a SNAT port with a cooldown timer.
solution: Associate a NAT gateway with the subnet (recommended), assign a public IP directly to the VM, or configure outbound rules on Standard Load Balancer to allocate more SNAT ports.

symptom: VNet peering status shows Disconnected - traffic cannot flow between peered virtual networks
rootCause: One of the two peering links was deleted (manual deletion, address space changes without sync, subscription/tenant changes, or Azure Policy enforcement). When one link is removed, the remaining link transitions to Disconnected.
solution: Delete the remaining peering link from BOTH virtual networks first, then re-create peering from both sides. Ensure correct settings for Allow forwarded traffic, Allow gateway transit, and Use remote gateways. Verify Connected on both sides.

symptom: Cannot create VNet peering - error: Virtual network address space overlaps with the address space of the already peered virtual network
rootCause: Azure does not allow virtual network peering between two VNets with overlapping CIDR address spaces. Even partial overlap (e.g., 10.0.0.0/16 and 10.0.1.0/24) is blocked.
solution: Resize one of the virtual networks to remove the overlapping address range (ensure no subnets/resources in the range). Alternatively use Azure Virtual Network Manager. Plan address spaces before peering using centralized IPAM.

symptom: Point-to-site VPN clients cannot reach spoke VNet resources after establishing or changing VNet peering in hub-spoke topology
rootCause: Point-to-site VPN clients cache routes at connection time. When VNet peering is established or modified, the P2S client package does not automatically pick up new routes to spoke virtual networks.
solution: After establishing or changing VNet peering, download and reinstall the point-to-site client package so P2S clients get updated routes. Configure Allow gateway transit on hub VNet and Use remote gateways on spoke VNet.

symptom: Traffic to newly added address ranges in peered VNet fails after address space change - effective routes still show old address prefixes
rootCause: After modifying address space of a peered VNet, the peering must be synced to update remote peer routing. Without sync, remote peered VNet uses old address space, causing routing failures.
solution: Sync peering after every address space change: Azure portal > VNet > Peering > select > Sync. CLI: az network vnet peering sync. Repeat for each remote peer. Note: sync not supported when peered with classic VNet.

symptom: DNS name resolution fails across peered VNets - nslookup returns NXDOMAIN for VM hostnames in peered VNet but ping by IP works
rootCause: VNet peering provides network-layer connectivity only. Azure-provided DNS (168.63.129.16) resolves names only within the same VNet or linked Private DNS zones. Cross-VNet DNS not automatic.
solution: Option 1 (recommended): Create Azure Private DNS zone, link to both peered VNets with auto-registration enabled. Option 2: Configure custom DNS server with conditional forwarding to 168.63.129.16. Restart VMs or renew DHCP lease after DNS changes.

symptom: Cannot delete Azure virtual network - error Subnet is in use or cannot be deleted because in use by resource
rootCause: Resources attached to the VNet prevent deletion: VNet gateways, application gateways, VMs, container instances, Entra Domain Services, peerings, or VNet stuck in migration state.
solution: Follow deletion order: 1) Gateway connections 2) Gateways 3) Public IPs 4) VNet peerings 5) ASE. Check Connected devices in VNet overview. For migration stuck: Move-AzureVirtualNetwork -Abort. Verify no VMs running.

symptom: Cannot delete Azure subnet - blocked by private endpoints, subnet delegation, service association links, or orphaned NICs
rootCause: Subnet deletion blocked by: private endpoints, active delegation (e.g., Microsoft.Web/serverFarms), service association links, orphaned NICs, or Azure Bastion/Firewall reserved subnets.
solution: Diagnose with: az network vnet subnet show --query {delegations,privateEndpoints,ipConfigurations,serviceAssociationLinks}. Remove private endpoints first, then service resources, then delegation. Orphaned NICs: delete NIC. Service association links may take 10-15 min to clear.

symptom: Cannot resize subnet - error when new address prefix excludes IP addresses currently in use by VMs, load balancers, or other resources
rootCause: Azure requires the new subnet address range to include ALL existing IP allocations. If the new prefix excludes any assigned IP, the resize fails. Azure also reserves 5 IPs per subnet (first 4 + last).
solution: Check used IPs: az network vnet subnet show --query ipConfigurations. Ensure new prefix covers all assigned IPs plus 5 reserved. Move resources to another subnet if shrinking is needed.

symptom: P2S VPN Error 798 - A certificate could not be found that can be used with this Extensible Authentication Protocol
rootCause: Client certificate (.pfx) not installed in Current User Personal Certificates store, or root certificate (.cer) not in Local Computer Trusted Root Certification Authorities.
solution: Open certmgr.msc. Verify AzureClient.pfx in Current User Personal Certificates, AzureRoot.cer in Local Computer Trusted Root CA. Re-import if missing. Do NOT enable strong private key protection when importing.

symptom: P2S VPN Error 0x80090326 - The message received was unexpected or badly formatted
rootCause: Root certificate public key not uploaded to Azure VPN gateway, key is corrupted/expired, or UDR with default route 0.0.0.0/0 on GatewaySubnet disrupts tunnel establishment.
solution: 1) Remove UDR on GatewaySubnet. 2) Check root cert in Azure portal VPN gateway Point-to-site Root certificates. 3) Delete and re-upload root cert if revoked/expired. 4) Re-download VPN client package.

symptom: P2S IKEv2 VPN remote server not responding on Windows 10/Server 2016 - connection cannot be established
rootCause: Windows version lacks IKE fragmentation support. Without specific KB updates and registry key, IKEv2 connections fail when large IKE packets cannot be fragmented.
solution: Install KB: Win Server 2016/Win10 1607=KB4057142, Win10 1703=KB4057144, Win10 1709=KB4089848. Set registry HKLM SYSTEM CurrentControlSet Services RasMan IKEv2 DisableCertReqPayload=1 (DWORD). Pre-Win10 must use SSTP.

symptom: P2S VPN connected but cannot access SMB network file shares - Kerberos authentication fails over VPN
rootCause: VPN client connects from Internet, cannot reach domain controller for Kerberos. Windows caches domain creds and forces Kerberos over NTLM. Without DC access, KDC_ERR_C_PRINCIPAL_UNKNOWN returned.
solution: Disable domain credential caching: Set registry HKLM SYSTEM CurrentControlSet Control Lsa DisableDomainCreds=1 to force NTLM fallback. Or ensure DC is reachable over VPN tunnel.

symptom: P2S VPN client cannot resolve FQDNs of on-premises resources - DNS queries fail for local domain names
rootCause: P2S VPN client uses Azure DNS servers configured on the VNet which take precedence over local DNS. Azure DNS 168.63.129.16 has no records for on-prem resources.
solution: Configure DNS Forwarders or Conditional Forwarders on Azure DNS for on-prem domains. Or use Azure Private Resolver with forwarding rules. Verify VPN adapter DNS metric.

symptom: P2S VPN Error 0x80092013 - revocation function unable to check revocation, revocation server offline
rootCause: Client cannot reach CRL endpoints crl3.digicert.com and crl4.digicert.com. Proxy server blocks direct HTTP access or edge firewall denies non-proxied requests.
solution: Configure proxy to allow access to CRL URLs. Whitelist crl3.digicert.com and crl4.digicert.com on edge firewall. Ensure system proxy is set correctly for VPN client process.

symptom: P2S VPN Error 812 - connection prevented because of policy configured on RAS/VPN server (RADIUS)
rootCause: RADIUS server used for VPN authentication has incorrect settings: NAS port type, network policy, or shared secret mismatch between Azure VPN Gateway and RADIUS server.
solution: Verify RADIUS config: correct shared secret, NAS port type=Virtual (VPN), network policy matches VPN client attributes. Check Azure MFA Server integration if using multi-factor.

symptom: P2S VPN profile repeatedly deleted and recreated on Windows 11 causing disconnections during Intune sync
rootCause: Intune compares assigned VPN profile XML with device current profile. Windows regenerates XML with different formatting. Differences cause Intune to delete and re-provision profile.
solution: Export working profile XML from device: Get-CimInstance -Namespace root/cimv2/mdm/dmmap -ClassName MDM_VPNv2_01, WriteAllText to file. Use Windows-generated XML as Intune profile to ensure format consistency.

symptom: P2S VPN clients cannot resolve Private DNS zone records including private endpoints - nslookup returns NXDOMAIN
rootCause: Azure DNS IP 168.63.129.16 is only resolvable from Azure platform resources, not from P2S VPN clients directly. P2S clients cannot reach wire server for Private DNS resolution.
solution: Deploy Azure Private Resolver. Configure inbound endpoint IP as custom DNS on VNet instead of 168.63.129.16. VPN gateway pushes custom DNS to P2S clients. Ensure Private DNS zones linked to VNet with Private Resolver.

symptom: macOS P2S IKEv2 VPN connection fails using native macOS VPN client - unable to establish tunnel
rootCause: Incorrect VPN settings on macOS: Server Address not full FQDN with cloudapp.net, Remote ID not matching Server Address, Local ID not matching client certificate Subject, or wrong auth method.
solution: Verify: Server Address=full FQDN with cloudapp.net, Remote ID=same as Server Address, Local ID=client cert Subject, Auth=Certificate. Requires macOS 10.11+. Debug with Wireshark filter isakmp to check SA proposal match.

symptom: S2S VPN connection suddenly stops working and cannot reconnect - tunnel was previously functional
rootCause: Shared key (PSK) mismatch between Azure VPN gateway and on-premises VPN device after configuration changes on either side.
solution: Compare keys: Azure portal > VPN Gateway > Connections > Authentication. PowerShell: Get-AzVirtualNetworkGatewayConnectionSharedKey. Verify on-prem device uses identical key. Also check Local Network Gateway IP matches on-prem device.

symptom: S2S VPN tunnel fails or drops - UDR or NSG on GatewaySubnet blocks IPsec/IKE traffic
rootCause: User-defined routes or NSGs on GatewaySubnet redirect or block VPN tunnel traffic. Any routing/filtering on GatewaySubnet disrupts IPsec/IKE negotiations.
solution: Remove UDR and NSG from GatewaySubnet. Test connectivity. If UDR/NSG needed, ensure UDP 500, 4500 and ESP protocol not affected. Never route 0.0.0.0/0 away from GatewaySubnet.

symptom: S2S VPN intermittent disconnections - tunnel drops periodically and re-establishes
rootCause: Perfect Forward Secrecy (PFS) enabled on on-prem device with mismatched settings on Azure gateway. Also caused by on-prem device external IP included in Local Network definition creating routing loop.
solution: 1) Disable PFS on on-prem device or match PFS settings on Azure IPsec policy. 2) Remove on-prem device external IP from Local Network address space. 3) Check health probe: https://<GatewayIP>:8081/healthprobe. 4) Reset gateway if needed.

symptom: S2S VPN policy-based gateway traffic not flowing despite tunnel connected - traffic selector mismatch
rootCause: Policy-based VPN requires exact subnet match between Azure VNet address spaces and on-prem definitions. Any mismatch causes traffic selectors to fail silently.
solution: Verify VNet address spaces match on-prem remote network definitions exactly. Local Network Gateway subnets must match on-prem local definitions exactly. Consider migrating to route-based gateway for flexible routing.

symptom: S2S VPN IKE negotiation timeout (error 13805) - on-premises VPN device not responding to Azure VPN gateway IKE messages
rootCause: On-premises VPN device IP incorrectly configured on Local Network Gateway, or on-premises firewall blocking IKE packets, or on-premises device not responding to IKE messages.
solution: 1) Verify on-premises IP on Local Network Gateway resource. 2) Check if on-premises device receives IKE packets. 3) Check on-premises firewall rules for IKE. 4) Review on-premises VPN device logs. 5) Engage device vendor if needed.

symptom: S2S VPN pre-shared key mismatch (error 13801) - IKE authentication credentials are unacceptable
rootCause: Pre-shared key configured on Azure connection resource does not match the key configured on the on-premises VPN device tunnel.
solution: Verify and ensure the pre-shared key on the Azure connection resource matches exactly with the on-premises VPN device tunnel configuration.

symptom: S2S VPN IKE/IPsec policy mismatch (error 13868/13825) or DH computation failure (error 13822) - policy match error or no policy configured
rootCause: IKE/IPsec policy or DH group configuration on Azure connection resource does not match the on-premises VPN device tunnel configuration.
solution: For custom policy: ensure IKE policy on on-premises device matches Azure connection. For default policy: check IPsec/IKE connection policies documentation. Verify DH group matches on both sides. Engage VPN device vendor if DH computation fails.

symptom: S2S VPN traffic selector mismatch (error 13999) - traffic selectors unacceptable by on-premises device
rootCause: Traffic selector configuration proposed by Azure VPN gateway is not accepted by on-premises device due to configuration mismatch.
solution: 1) Check on-premises device logs for traffic selector rejection reason. 2) Fix traffic selector config on on-premises device, or 3) Configure policy-based traffic selector on Azure connection to match on-premises device.

symptom: S2S VPN invalid IKE messages or version mismatch (error 13824/13843/13846/13880) - unsupported protocol from on-premises device
rootCause: On-premises VPN device sending unsupported IKE messages/protocols, or IKE version (IKEv1 vs IKEv2) mismatch between Azure connection and on-premises device.
solution: 1) Ensure on-premises device is a validated VPN device. 2) Ensure IKE version matches on both sides. 3) Contact on-premises device vendor for help.

symptom: S2S VPN connection refused (error 1225) - remote computer refused the network connection when on-premises device initiates
rootCause: Azure connection resource is configured as Initiator only mode, so it does not accept incoming connection requests from on-premises device.
solution: Update the connection mode property on Azure connection resource to Default or Responder only.

symptom: VPN Gateway digital certificate authentication failures - certificate expired, invalid, missing private key, duplicate, or chain build errors
rootCause: Root/intermediate certificate issues: expired certificate, certificate contains private key (only public key allowed), duplicate entries, invalid X.509 format, chain creation failure, or outbound certificate missing RSA private key / insufficient key length.
solution: 1) Replace expired certificates with valid ones. 2) Use only public key portion for root certs. 3) Remove duplicate entries. 4) Verify X.509 format. 5) Ensure outbound cert has RSA private key of required length. 6) Ensure outbound cert has both client and server authentication in EKU. 7) Max 5 certs in chain (root + intermediate).

symptom: VPN Gateway Key Vault access failures - token request failed, secret not found, keyvault inaccessible, request timeout, or DNS resolution failure
rootCause: VPN Gateway cannot access Key Vault due to: missing managed identity properties, secret/certificate not found, Key Vault inaccessible, request timeout, or Key Vault host URL cannot be resolved (DNS issue).
solution: 1) Verify Key Vault accessibility and configuration. 2) Verify secret/certificate exists with correct name. 3) Check outbound certificate URL path in CertificateAuthentication object. 4) Verify managed identity has proper Key Vault access. 5) For token/timeout issues, retry and open support ticket if persistent.

symptom: VPN Gateway managed identity or authentication type misconfiguration - wrong identity type, multiple identities, or PSK/cert conflict
rootCause: Using non-UserAssigned identity type (only UserAssigned supported), assigning multiple identities (only one allowed), CertificateAuthentication set when using PSK auth, SharedKey set when using cert auth, or trying to delete identity still in use by connection.
solution: 1) Use only UserAssigned identity type. 2) Assign only one identity per gateway. 3) Remove CertificateAuthentication when using PSK. 4) Remove SharedKey/SharedKeyKeyvaultId when using cert auth. 5) Remove identity from connections before deleting it. 6) VpnGw1+ required for managed identity.

symptom: ExpressRoute gateway migration fails at Validate stage - GatewaySubnet too small for deploying second gateway
rootCause: Virtual network GatewaySubnet does not have sufficient address space to deploy another gateway during migration.
solution: Delete and recreate the GatewaySubnet as /27 or shorter prefix (e.g., /26 or /25) before initiating migration.

symptom: ExpressRoute gateway migration blocked by legacy connections (pre-2017) or resources in failed state
rootCause: Legacy connection mode: connections created before 2017 are incompatible with migration. Failed state: gateway or connected resources not in Succeeded provisioning state.
solution: For legacy connections: delete and recreate all connections before migration. For failed state: perform GET+SET on affected resources to restore Succeeded state. Default SKU must be upgraded to Standard; FastPath and route weight must not change during migration.

symptom: ExpressRoute circuit stuck in failed provisioning state after an operation does not complete successfully
rootCause: An Azure operation on the ExpressRoute circuit did not complete, leaving the circuit resource in a failed state.
solution: Reset the circuit: in Azure portal, open the circuit and click Refresh; or via PowerShell: $ckt = Get-AzExpressRouteCircuit -Name '<name>' -ResourceGroupName '<rg>'; Set-AzExpressRouteCircuit -ExpressRouteCircuit $ckt. If still failed, open a Microsoft support ticket.

symptom: ExpressRoute ARP table shows on-premises MAC address as Incomplete or on-premises entry is missing entirely
rootCause: Layer 2 connectivity problem on the on-premises or connectivity provider side — the on-premises router is not responding to ARP requests from the MSEE.
solution: 1) Verify the first IP of the /30 subnet is used on MSEE-PR interface (Azure uses the second IP for MSEE). 2) Check customer (C-Tag) and service (S-Tag) VLAN tags match on both MSEE-PR and MSEE pair. 3) Open a support request with your connectivity provider.

symptom: ExpressRoute ARP table for a peering does not appear at all (empty or no output)
rootCause: Layer 2 connectivity issue on the Microsoft side — MSEE is not generating ARP entries for the peering.
solution: Open a support ticket with Microsoft support specifying a layer 2 connectivity issue. Provide the output of Get-AzExpressRouteCircuitARPTable for both primary and secondary paths.

symptom: NVA (Network Virtual Appliance) with Standard SKU public IP not receiving traffic - inbound packets do not reach the appliance despite correct UDR routing
rootCause: Standard SKU public IPs deny all inbound traffic by default unless an NSG with explicit allow rules is associated. Unlike Basic SKU, Standard requires mandatory NSG.
solution: Create an NSG and associate it with the NVA NIC or subnet. Add explicit inbound allow rules for the required traffic (e.g., TCP 80/443). Also verify IP forwarding is enabled on NVA NIC and UDR next hop points to NVA IP.

symptom: Azure VM cannot connect to the internet - network interface (NIC) stuck in failed provisioning state after a previous operation did not complete successfully
rootCause: A previous ARM operation on the network interface failed, leaving the NIC resource in a Failed provisioning state. The NIC cannot process traffic in this state.
solution: Use Azure Resource Explorer (resources.azure.com): 1) Find the affected NIC under Microsoft.Network/networkInterfaces. 2) Switch to Read/Write mode. 3) Click Edit then Put to trigger re-provisioning. 4) Wait for ProvisioningState to change from Updating to Succeeded. Then refresh Azure portal to confirm.

symptom: Outbound SMTP on TCP port 25 is blocked from Azure VM - emails to external domains (outlook.com, gmail.com) fail to send
rootCause: Azure platform blocks outbound SMTP connections on port 25 for all subscription types except Enterprise Agreement and MCA-E. This is a platform-level restriction to protect IP reputation and prevent spam.
solution: Use authenticated SMTP relay services on port 587 (e.g., Azure Communication Services, SendGrid, Office 365 SMTP relay). For Enterprise Agreement subscriptions, port 25 is not blocked but delivery depends on recipient domain acceptance. For Enterprise Dev/Test, request unblock via Azure portal Diagnose and Solve > Cannot send email (SMTP-Port 25), then stop/deallocate/restart all VMs.

symptom: VNet peering status shows Disconnected - traffic cannot flow between peered virtual networks
rootCause: One of the two peering links was deleted (manual deletion, address space change without sync, or subscription/tenant change). When one link is removed, the remaining link transitions to Disconnected state.
solution: Delete the remaining peering link from both virtual networks (az network vnet peering delete), then recreate peering from both sides. Cannot re-create while one side is Disconnected - must delete both first. After recreation, verify status shows Connected on both sides.

symptom: Cannot create VNet peering - error: address space overlaps with already peered virtual network
rootCause: Azure does not allow virtual network peering between two VNets with overlapping CIDR address spaces. Any intersection of IP ranges blocks the peering operation.
solution: 1) Identify overlapping ranges: az network vnet show --query addressSpace.addressPrefixes for both VNets. 2) Resize one VNet to remove overlap (ensure no subnets/resources in the range being removed). 3) For future prevention, use centralized IPAM. Alternative: use Azure Virtual Network Manager with ConnectedGroupAddressOverlap=Disallowed.

symptom: Point-to-site VPN clients cannot reach spoke VNet after establishing or changing VNet peering with hub
rootCause: P2S VPN client route tables are not automatically updated when new peerings are established or modified. The client retains stale routes that do not include the spoke VNet address space.
solution: After establishing or changing VNet peering: 1) Configure VPN gateway transit on the peering. 2) Download and reinstall the P2S client package on all VPN clients so they get the updated routes to the spoke VNet.

symptom: VMs in newly peered VNets cannot reach each other immediately after peering creation - effective routes do not show peered VNet address space
rootCause: Azure route propagation for VNet peering system routes takes 2-5 minutes to complete. During this window, VMs do not have routes to the peered VNet.
solution: Wait 2-5 minutes after peering creation. Then verify effective routes (az network nic show-effective-route-table) show entries with next hop type Virtual network peering. If routes do not appear after 5 min: verify peering Connected on both sides, check address spaces do not overlap, check no Azure Policy blocking propagation.

symptom: After adding/modifying address ranges in a peered VNet, traffic to the new ranges fails - remote peer still shows old address space
rootCause: VNet peering does not automatically sync address space changes. The remote peer continues using old address prefixes until a manual sync is performed.
solution: Sync the peering after every address space change: Azure portal > VNet > Peering > select peering > Sync. Or CLI: az network vnet peering sync. Repeat for each remote peered VNet. Note: sync is not supported when peered with classic VNet. Verify with az network vnet peering show --query remoteAddressSpace.

symptom: DNS name resolution fails across peered virtual networks - VM can ping peer IP but hostname resolution returns NXDOMAIN
rootCause: VNet peering provides network-layer connectivity only. Azure default DNS does not resolve private hostnames across peered VNets - it only works within the same VNet or linked Private DNS zones.
solution: Option 1 (recommended): Create Azure Private DNS zone, link to both peered VNets with auto-registration enabled. VMs resolve via <hostname>.<zone> format. Option 2: Configure custom DNS server with conditional forwarding to Azure DNS 168.63.129.16 for the peered VNet zone. After DNS changes, restart VMs or renew DHCP lease.

symptom: Cannot delete virtual network - error indicates VNet is in use by resources
rootCause: Dependent resources still exist in the VNet: VPN/ExpressRoute gateway, Application Gateway, VMs, VNet peerings, Container Instances, Microsoft Entra Domain Services, or VNet stuck in migration state.
solution: Delete resources in order: 1) Gateway connections 2) Gateways 3) Public IPs 4) VNet peerings 5) App Service Environments 6) VMs and NICs. For migration-stuck VNets: Move-AzureVirtualNetwork -VirtualNetworkName <name> -Abort. Check Connected devices in VNet Overview page to identify remaining resources.

symptom: Cannot delete or modify subnet - error: subnet is in use / has delegations / has service association links
rootCause: Subnet has blocking resources: private endpoints, subnet delegations (e.g. Microsoft.Sql/managedInstances), service association links from PaaS deployments, orphaned NICs, or reserved subnets (AzureBastionSubnet, AzureFirewallSubnet, GatewaySubnet).
solution: Diagnose: az network vnet subnet show --query '{delegations, privateEndpoints, ipConfigurations, serviceAssociationLinks}'. Delete in order: 1) VMs and NICs 2) Private endpoints 3) Service deployments 4) Wait for service association links cleanup (10-15 min) 5) Remove delegation (az network vnet subnet update --remove delegations) 6) Delete subnet.

symptom: BGP peer status shows Unknown or Not connected on Azure VPN Gateway - dynamic routing not working
rootCause: Common causes: 1) IPsec S2S tunnel not established (BGP runs over IPsec). 2) ASN mismatch between Azure and on-premises. 3) On-premises device peers with gateway public IP instead of BGP peer IP. 4) Using reserved ASNs (8074, 8075, 12076, 65515-65520). 5) Basic SKU or Policy-based VPN not supported.
solution: 1) Verify S2S tunnel Connected first. 2) Check Azure BGP ASN in gateway Configuration page. 3) Ensure on-premises peers with Azure BGP peer IP (from GatewaySubnet), not public IP. 4) Verify ASNs are different and not reserved. 5) For APIPA peers: use range 169.254.21.0-169.254.22.255. Enable RouteDiagnosticLog for BGP events.

symptom: BGP session on Azure VPN Gateway drops suddenly and repeatedly - on-premises routes disappear then reappear
rootCause: On-premises VPN device advertises more than 4000 prefixes per BGP peer. Azure VPN Gateway drops the entire BGP session when this limit is exceeded. Session re-establishes when fewer prefixes are advertised, creating a flapping pattern.
solution: Reduce on-premises route advertisements to under 4000 prefixes per peer. Use route summarization/aggregation on the on-premises router. Monitor with RouteDiagnosticLog: query BgpDisconnectedEvent for patterns. Note: BFD is not supported on VPN Gateway S2S; keep-alive=60s, hold timer=180s are fixed values.

symptom: Azure VPN Gateway not advertising VNet address space routes to on-premises peer via BGP despite session being Connected
rootCause: When VNet peering with gateway transit is enabled, Azure blocks advertisement of prefixes that exactly match the VNet address space. Only superset prefixes can be advertised (e.g., 10.0.0.0/8 instead of 10.0.0.0/16).
solution: Check advertised routes: Get-AzVirtualNetworkGatewayAdvertisedRoute. If expected VNet prefix is missing due to gateway transit duplicate prefix restriction, configure a superset prefix on the on-premises side. Verify on-premises router has no inbound route filters blocking Azure prefixes. Check received routes appear in on-premises routing table.

symptom: Azure Front Door compression enabled for route but files returned uncompressed (no Content-Encoding header in response)
rootCause: Content not eligible for compression, compression not enabled for the file type, HTTP request missing Accept-Encoding header, or origin sending chunked content. IIS origins may refuse compression when Via header is present (proxy scenario).
solution: Verify request goes to *.azurefd.net (not direct to origin) and includes Accept-Encoding header. Confirm compression enabled in route settings (Azure portal). Wait 1-2 hours after first setup for POP propagation. For IIS 6: set HcNoCompressionForProxies=FALSE; for IIS 7+: set noCompressionForHttp10 and noCompressionForProxies to False.

symptom: Azure Front Door returns 503 or 504 after ~30 seconds. Direct requests to origin succeed. Intermittent 503 with ErrorInfo: OriginInvalidResponse.
rootCause: Origin response exceeds default 30-second timeout, or client sends byte range requests with Accept-Encoding header causing origin to respond with mismatched content lengths.
solution: Increase Origin response timeout (up to 240 sec, Standard/Premium only) in Front Door profile overview page. For byte range + Accept-Encoding conflict: disable compression on origin/AFD, or create Rules Set rule to remove Accept-Encoding from byte range requests.

symptom: Azure Front Door returns 503 only for HTTPS endpoints. HTTP works fine. ErrorInfo: OriginInvalidResponse.
rootCause: Backend uses IP address without FQDN (no SNI match), backend certificate does not match FQDN, or Azure Web App uses IP-based SSL instead of SNI.
solution: Disable EnforceCertificateNameCheck if backend is IP-based (portal toggle or origin settings). Ensure returned certificate matches backend FQDN. For Web Apps: switch from IP-based to SNI SSL. Verify with: openssl s_client -connect backend:443 -servername backend.

symptom: Requests to Azure Front Door custom domain return HTTP 400, while *.azurefd.net subdomain works normally.
rootCause: No routing rule configured for the custom domain frontend host. A routing rule must be explicitly added even if one exists for the *.azurefd.net subdomain.
solution: Add a routing rule for the custom domain to direct traffic to the selected origin group.

symptom: Azure Front Door returns HTTP 411 with message: The request must be chunked or have a content length.
rootCause: HTTP request is not RFC 7230 compliant. Typically a POST request sent without Content-Length or Transfer-Encoding header (e.g. curl -X POST without body). Enforced regardless of WAF configuration.
solution: Ensure all HTTP requests comply with RFC 7230 - include Content-Length or Transfer-Encoding header in POST/PUT requests. Check the HTML error message body for specific noncompliance details.

symptom: Azure Front Door returns 429 Too Many Requests for a percentage of traffic.
rootCause: Traffic exceeds Azure Front Door default platform rate limits.
solution: Create an Azure support request to increase the quota limit for legitimate traffic.

symptom: Azure Front Door origin configured as IP address is healthy but rejects requests from Front Door.
rootCause: Front Door uses origin hostname as SNI header during SSL handshake. IP-based origin has no FQDN for SNI, and origin certificate logic may reject requests without a valid host header matching the certificate.
solution: Change origin from IP address to an FQDN with a valid certificate that matches the origin. This ensures proper SNI matching during SSL handshake.
